title: Kidnapped
author: Robert Louis Stevenson
overture: In the summer of 1751 David Balfour walks out of the Borders to claim his inheritance - and is kidnapped, wrecked off Mull, and driven the length of the Highlands. This map is his one long journey: the brig Covenant's voyage round the top of Scotland, the wreck on the Torran Rocks, and the flight in the heather with Alan Breck - over Rannoch Moor and Ben Alder, and down at last to the Forth and home.

sources.name: The National Library of Scotland - the Kidnapped route

regions.name: The Lowlands - Edinburgh and the Forth

chapters.title: I Set Off Upon My Journey to the House of Shaws

I Go to the Queen's Ferry
What Befell at the Queen's Ferry
I Go to Sea in the Brig Covenant of Dysart

The Man with the Belt of Gold
The Siege of the Round-House
The Captain Knuckles Under
I Hear of the “Red Fox”
The Loss of the Brig
The Islet
The Lad with the Silver Button: Through the Isle of Mull
The Lad with the Silver Button: Across Morven
The Death of the Red Fox
I Talk with Alan in the Wood of Lettermore
The House of Fear
The Flight in the Heather: The Rocks
The Flight in the Heather: The Heugh of Corrynakiegh
The Flight in the Heather: The Moor

The Flight in the Heather: The Quarrel

Good-bye

characters.name: David Balfour
characters.role: The narrator and spine of the whole book: a newly-orphaned Lowland lad who walks out to claim his kin, is kidnapped and shipwrecked, and is driven the length of the Highlands before winning his inheritance in Edinburgh.

characters.name: Alan Breck Stewart
characters.role: A Jacobite exile and Appin Stewart, gun-runner for the exiled clan chief, who becomes David's companion and protector through the flight in the heather.

locations.name: The Pentlands / the Borders
locations.note: David's home village, fictional and only loosely placed. Stevenson gives a 'quiet country-side' with a kirk and manse, a day or two's walk from Edinburgh; the standard 'Kidnapped trail' places it vaguely in the Borders south-west of the capital. Coordinates are a nominal Pentlands placing.
locations.story: The quiet parish where David is born and orphaned, and which he leaves one June morning in 1751 with a letter that sends him to claim his inheritance at the House of Shaws.
locations.quote: I will begin the story of my adventures with a certain morning early in the month of June, the year of grace 1751

image.caption: A lonely house on a Lowland brae, c.1890 - an indicative view of the quiet upland country-side of Essendean, David's invented home parish south-west of Edinburgh
image.credit: Robert W. Little, c.1890 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: near Cramond, west of Edinburgh
locations.note: The house is fictional - a grim, half-built mansion - but Stevenson expressly sites it near Cramond on the Firth of Forth, west of Edinburgh. No single real building is agreed; the district is real, the exact spot a best guess.
locations.story: The blighted, half-finished house of David's miserly uncle Ebenezer, who first tries to kill him on a broken stair in the dark, then has him kidnapped. David returns at the end to trap the old man into confessing.
locations.quote: give my boy this letter into his hand, and start him off to the house of Shaws, not far from Cramond

image.caption: Borthwick Castle, a gaunt Lowland tower house, engraved in the late 18th century - an indicative stand-in for the grim, half-built house of Shaws, which Stevenson invented but sited near Cramond
image.credit: Engraving by P. Mazell, late 18th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: South Queensferry (the Hawes Inn)
locations.note: Precisely real. South Queensferry on the Firth of Forth, where the Hawes Inn still stands (now beneath the Forth Bridge). David is decoyed aboard the brig Covenant here. Stevenson knew the inn; coordinates are for it.
locations.story: The ferry-town on the Forth where Ebenezer walks David down 'on business' and, at the Hawes Inn, has him struck down and carried aboard the Covenant. David returns here at the end to find the lawyer Rankeillor.
locations.quote: The Hawes Inn, at the Queen's Ferry

image.caption: The Queensferry from the south-east, with the Firth of Forth and a coach on the shore road, engraved 1843
image.credit: William Miller after Clarkson Stanfield, 1843 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The Torran Rocks, off the Ross of Mull
locations.note: A real, notoriously dangerous reef stretching south and west off the Ross of Mull. The Covenant strikes here in the dark. The reef spreads over miles, so the single point is representative but the feature is real and charted.
locations.story: The reef, off the south-west end of Mull near Iona, where the brig Covenant is thrown by the tide and beats herself to pieces - scattering the crew and casting David up alone.
locations.quote: I'm thinking," said Alan, "these'll be what they call the Torran Rocks
locations.imageBlank: No period view of the Torran Rocks reef itself exists; the one candidate is a bright 1920s beach study whose calm undercuts the Covenant's wreck - a blank is more honest.

locations.name: Erraid (Eilean Earraid)
locations.note: A real tidal islet off the south-west tip of the Ross of Mull, near Iona. Stevenson knew it personally - his family's lighthouse firm had a shore station here. David is marooned until he realises at low tide he can wade across.
locations.story: The little tidal islet where David is cast ashore alone after the wreck, and spends wretched days believing himself trapped - until he grasps that the sea goes out and he can simply walk off.
locations.quote: in under the southwest end of Mull, off a little isle they call Earraid

image.caption: The ruins of Iona Cathedral from the north-west, c.1860 - Iona lies just off Earraid at the south-west tip of the Ross of Mull, the very corner where David is cast ashore
image.credit: E. J. Bedford, c.1860 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Isle of Mull (the Ross of Mull)
locations.note: Real island. After escaping Earraid, David crosses the Ross of Mull on foot - 'fifty miles as the crow might fly' - east toward Torosay and the ferry to the mainland. Coordinates are a central point on the Ross.
locations.story: The long, crooked island David treks across on Alan's silver-button token, guided and half-swindled by the folk of the Ross, working east toward the Appin country.
locations.quote: big and crooked Island of Mull, from Earraid to Torosay, fifty miles as the crow flies

image.caption: Fishing smacks off Staffa in choppy seas, with Iona in the distance, 1853 - the Hebridean waters at the south-west end of Mull where David is wrecked
image.credit: Anthony Vandyke Copley Fielding, 1853 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Kinlochaline and Morvern
locations.note: Real. David takes the ferry from Torosay across the Sound of Mull to Kinlochaline on the Morvern peninsula, then crosses it. Kinlochaline is a precise real location; Morvern a real district.
locations.story: The mainland shore David is ferried to across the Sound of Mull, and the wild peninsula of Morvern he crosses, still following Alan's trail toward Appin.
locations.quote: There is a regular ferry from Torosay to Kinlochaline on the mainland

image.caption: Kinlochaline Castle at the head of Loch Aline in Morvern, painted by Horatio McCulloch in 1855 - the mainland shore where David steps off the ferry from Mull and sets out across the Highlands.
image.credit: Horatio McCulloch, 'Kinlochaline Castle, Morvern', 1855 · Kelvingrove Art Gallery · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Kingairloch and Ardgour
locations.note: Real districts on the mainland opposite Mull. Crossing east, David comes down to the shore of Loch Linnhe near Kingairloch, where he takes a boat over into Appin.
locations.story: The Loch Linnhe shore where David, near the end of his lonely trek, takes a boat across into Appin - and walks straight into the killing that changes everything.
locations.quote: our ways lying together as far as to Kingairloch

image.caption: Loch Linnhe near Fort William, painted on the spot in 1848 - the deep, still sea-loch David comes down to and is rowed across into Appin

locations.name: Lettermore, Appin (the Appin Murder)
locations.note: Real and commemorated. In the wood of Lettermore near Ballachulish, Colin Roy Campbell of Glenure - the 'Red Fox', the King's factor - is shot dead by an unseen marksman: the real 1752 Appin Murder (Stevenson pins it to 1751), for which James Stewart was hanged. A cairn marks the spot; coordinates are for the monument.
locations.story: The Appin wood where David, arriving at the worst possible moment, sees the Red Fox shot from the hillside - and, marked as a suspect, falls in with Alan Breck and takes to his heels. Here the flight begins.
locations.quote: there came the shot of a firelock from higher up the hill; and with the very sound of it Glenure fell upon the road

image.caption: Glen Coe, in the Appin country near Ballachulish, engraved after Turner in 1836 - the brooding Highland ground of the wood of Lettermore, where the Red Fox, Colin Campbell, is shot dead in the Appin Murder.
image.credit: William Miller after J. M. W. Turner, 'Glen Coe', 1836 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Corrynakiegh, Appin (Glen Duror hills)
locations.note: A rocky cleft where David and Alan lie up for five days after the murder. Not precisely fixed; trail-followers associate it with the Glen Duror / Coire hills of Appin. Fictionalised or lightly renamed; position approximate.
locations.story: The hidden cleft in the Appin hills where the two shelter, fish for trout, and signal for money and news, while the redcoats scour the country below for the murderers.
locations.quote: The name of the cleft was the Heugh of Corrynakiegh

image.caption: A Highland glen and sea-loch in Argyll, 1867 - an indicative view of the Appin hill country where the invented cleft of Corrynakiegh hides the two fugitives
image.credit: Robert S. Duncanson, 1867 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Rannoch Moor
locations.note: Real, and one of Britain's largest expanses of blanket bog. The great desolate waste crossed in the flight in the heather, where David nearly collapses. Coordinates are the centre of the moor.
locations.story: The vast, skeleton-firred bog the fugitives cross by crawling from heather-bush to heather-bush under a blazing sun, in full view of the soldiers on the surrounding hills - the set-piece of the whole flight.
locations.quote: there was quite a forest of dead firs, standing like skeletons. A wearier-looking desert man never saw

image.caption: The Moor of Rannoch, painted 1868 - the great desolate bog the fugitives crawl across in full view of the soldiers
image.credit: Andrew MacCallum, 1868 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Ben Alder (Cluny's Cage)
locations.note: Ben Alder is a real, precisely-locatable mountain; Cluny's Cage - the wattled hideout where the real fugitive chief Cluny Macpherson sheltered them - has no exactly-agreed spot, traditionally placed on the wooded flank above Loch Ericht. Coordinates are the Ben Alder summit as anchor; the Cage itself is approximate.
locations.story: The mountain into whose 'heart' the flight drives them, where Cluny Macpherson's scouts take them up to his wicker hiding-place. Feverish, David quarrels bitterly with Alan over money gambled away.
locations.quote: into the heart of that dismal mountain of Ben Alder

image.caption: A storm breaking over a Highland mountain pass (Glen Coe), engraved 1836 after Turner - an indicative view of the wild mountain fastness of Ben Alder, into whose heart the flight drives David and Alan
image.credit: William Miller after J. M. W. Turner, 1836 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Balquhidder
locations.note: Real - the MacGregor country in the braes of Balquhidder, where the piping-duel between Alan and Robin Oig (a son of the historical Rob Roy) takes place. Rob Roy's grave is in the kirkyard.
locations.story: The glen where David falls sick and the two rest, and where Alan out-plays Robin Oig, one of Rob Roy's sons, on the pipes rather than fight him. When David mends, the flight resumes.
locations.quote: the western end of the country of Balquhidder

image.caption: November on the Braes of Balquhidder - the MacGregor country where David falls sick and Alan out-pipes a son of Rob Roy
image.credit: Thomas Hunt, 1914 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Stirling (the old Bridge of Stirling)
locations.note: Real. The fugitives must cross the Forth; Stirling Bridge is 'the main pass over that river'. Foiled by the sentry, they slip east down the Ochils to find a boat lower down. Coordinates for the Old Bridge of Stirling.
locations.story: The guarded bridge that 'bridles the wild Hielandman' - where Alan's ruse fails to get them across, and they must turn east and downstream to steal a passage over the Forth.
locations.quote: Stirling Bridge, which is the main pass over that river

image.caption: Stirling Castle on its rock above the Forth, painted c.1840 - the town guarding the bridge that 'bridles the wild Hielandman'
image.credit: Montague Stanley, c.1840 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Limekilns, on the Forth
locations.note: Real village on the north shore of the Forth, looking across the Hope to Queensferry. Here a serving-girl, moved by pity, steals a boat and rows the fugitives across at night to the Lothian shore near Carriden. Precisely locatable.
locations.story: The little Forth-side clachan where a change-house girl, taking David for a hunted gentleman, rows them over the water single-handed by night - the quiet, kindly climax of the flight.
locations.quote: came to the little clachan of Limekilns. This is a place that sits near in by the water-side
locations.imageBlank: The Forth crossing at Queensferry is already pictured elsewhere in the book, and Limekilns clachan has no period view of its own; left a considered blank rather than repeat the ferry scene.

locations.name: Edinburgh (the British Linen Company's bank)
locations.note: Real. The novel ends with David walking into Edinburgh to claim his fortune at the British Linen Company, a real bank founded in 1746. Coordinates for the Old Town.
locations.story: Journey's end: David, his inheritance won and Alan parted from him on the Corstorphine road, walks into the city 'to the very doors of the British Linen Company's bank' - rich, and heavy-hearted.
locations.quote: The hand of Providence brought me in my drifting to the very doors of the British Linen Company's bank

image.caption: Edinburgh Castle above the Grassmarket, photographed c.1865–85 - the Old Town David walks into at journey's end to claim his fortune
image.credit: George Washington Wilson, c.1865–85 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.note: David walks out of the Borders with the minister's letter and comes, footsore, to the grim house of Shaws.

via.name: the drove-road
via.note: up onto the green drove-road through the heather, north for Edinburgh

via.name: Colinton
via.note: "worked my way to the westward of the capital by Colinton"

via.name: the Glasgow road
via.note: out onto the Glasgow road, then into Cramond parish

movements.routeNote: David's two-day walk out of the Borders to Cramond: over the ford onto the drove-road, past the smoking city of Edinburgh, and round to the west of the capital by Colinton to the Shaws.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, chs. 1–2: over the ford and up to the drove-road; on the second day Edinburgh comes in sight and David works "to the westward of the capital by Colinton" onto the Glasgow road, and so into Cramond parish.

movements.note: Ebenezer walks David down to the Forth 'on business' with the shipmaster Hoseason - the bait for the trap.

via.name: the mouth of the Forth
via.note: the brig beats out to sea

via.name: the north-east coast
via.note: standing up the east coast of Scotland

via.name: the Pentland Firth
via.note: round the very top of Scotland, to shun the Channel

via.name: Cape Wrath
via.note: the north-west corner, then down among the isles

via.name: the Minch
via.note: standing down the west coast, off the Hebrides

via.name: the reefs off Mull
via.note: weaving toward Iona when the tide takes her

movements.routeNote: The Covenant's course, north about Scotland and down the west coast, exactly as the novel sets it.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, Ch. 7: Hoseason means 'to go north about Scotland, and come down through the... the west coast', to avoid the Channel - the long way round the top.
movements.note: Kidnapped aboard the brig Covenant, David is carried out to sea. Rather than risk the Channel, Hoseason stands north about Scotland and down the west coast, bound to sell him in the Carolinas - until the reefs off Mull end the voyage.
Thrown from the breaking brig, David is washed up alone on the little isle of Earraid.
Realising Earraid is tidal, David wades off and sets out east across Mull on Alan's silver-button token.

via.name: the Ross of Mull
via.note: east across "rugged and trackless" moorland toward Torosay

movements.routeNote: Off the tidal isle and east over the Ross of Mull - "rugged and trackless" hill country - following the line of the peninsula toward Torosay and the ferry.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 15: "The Ross of Mull, which I had now got upon, was rugged and trackless"; David crosses it eastward, guided from house to house, toward Torosay.

movements.note: Ferried over the Sound of Mull to Kinlochaline on the mainland.

via.name: Torosay
via.note: the ferry slip on the east shore of Mull

via.name: the Sound of Mull
via.note: rowed up the Sound, no wind, to Gaelic boat-songs

via.name: the mouth of Loch Aline
via.note: into the sea-loch for the Kinlochaline shore

movements.routeNote: The regular ferry from Torosay across and up the Sound of Mull to Kinlochaline, at the mouth of Loch Aline on the Morvern shore.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 16: "There is a regular ferry from Torosay to Kinlochaline on the mainland" - rowed for want of wind up the Sound, "In the mouth of Loch Aline we found..."

movements.note: Across the wild peninsula of Morvern toward Loch Linnhe and the Appin country.

via.name: across Morvern
via.note: over the wild peninsula toward the Loch Linnhe shore

movements.routeNote: On foot across the rough peninsula of Morvern, coming down to the shore of Loch Linnhe near Kingairloch.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 16: David crosses Morven on foot toward Kingairloch and the Linnhe Loch; the exact hill-line is inferred from the ground.

movements.note: A boat across Loch Linnhe into Appin - and David walks into the wood of Lettermore just as the Red Fox is shot.

via.name: Loch Linnhe
via.note: a fisher-boat across the deep, still sea-loch into Appin

movements.routeNote: A fisherman rows David straight across Loch Linnhe from the Morvern shore into Appin, sparing him the two public ferries.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 17: "a man who had a boat of his own and was to cross the Linnhe Loch that afternoon into Appin" - the sea "very deep and still."

movements.note: Marked men, David and Alan flee together and lie up five days in the cleft of Corrynakiegh while the redcoats scour Appin.
East to the great moor - the crawling, sun-blistered crossing of Rannoch, in full view of the troops on the hills.

via.name: the head of Glencoe
via.note: over the top of Glencoe, "the very coming of the soldiers"

via.name: the edge of the moor
via.note: onto "yon bald, naked, flat place" - the desert of Rannoch

movements.routeNote: Seven hours’ hard travelling east off the Appin range, over the head of Glencoe and out onto the great desert of Rannoch Moor.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 22: "we’ll can strike east... east, ye see, we have the muirs"; the moor is reached over the top of Glencoe (ch. 20) into "yon bald, naked, flat place."

movements.note: Driven up into Ben Alder, where Cluny Macpherson's men take them to the wattled hideout of Cluny's Cage.

via.name: the waste of Rannoch
via.note: crawling heather-bush to heather-bush, north-east under the dragoons’ eyes

movements.routeNote: The hares’ dash north-east across the open waste toward Ben Alder - "a wild, desert mountain full of hills and hollows" - crawling from bush to bush in full view of the troops.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 22: Alan points to a mountain "on the north-eastern sky... Its name is Ben Alder" and they strike for it across the moor. The mountain is named; the exact crawling line over the bog is a gesture.

movements.note: Put across the lochs by night and south to Balquhidder, quarrelling bitterly on the road; David falls sick and they rest.

via.name: across Loch Errick
via.note: "put across Loch Errocht under cloud of night"

via.name: the head of Loch Rannoch
via.note: down the eastern shore to a hiding-place near Loch Rannoch

via.name: across Loch Rannoch
via.note: "the gillie put us across Loch Rannoch in the dusk"

via.name: the eastern hills
via.note: Alan’s swifter eastern way, shunning the Glenorchy Campbells

via.name: the mountain-tops
via.note: over the high tops, three nights’ march down toward Balquhidder

movements.routeNote: Ferried across Loch Errick by night and down to Loch Rannoch, put across it in the dusk, then Alan’s swifter eastern way over the mountain-tops, down to the western end of Balquhidder.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, chs. 24–25: "Alan and I were put across Loch Errocht under cloud of night... down its eastern shore to... the head of Loch Rannoch"; "The gillie put us across Loch Rannoch in the dusk"; Alan chooses the eastern way, three nights’ march to "the western end of the country of Balquhidder."

movements.note: Down out of the Highlands to Stirling - only to find the bridge, the main pass over the Forth, held by a sentry.

via.name: Uam Var
via.note: a heather bush on the hillside, "the happiest ten hours of sleep"

via.name: Allan Water
via.note: struck the water and followed it down out of the hills

via.name: the Carse of Stirling
via.note: the whole carse "as flat as a pancake," the castle on its hill

movements.routeNote: Down out of the Highlands past Uam Var, striking Allan Water and following it down onto the Carse of Stirling, to the old Bridge of Stirling under the castle.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 26: a heather bush "on the hillside in Uam Var"; "we struck Allan Water, and followed it down... saw the whole Carse of Stirling underfoot"; on to "the Bridge of Stirling."

movements.note: Foiled at the bridge, they skirt east under the Ochils, past Alloa, to the clachan of Limekilns on the Forth.

via.name: under the Ochils
via.note: all night through the north side of the Carse, under the Ochil line

via.name: Alloa
via.note: skirted, kept clear of

via.name: Clackmannan
via.note: avoided in the dark

via.name: Culross
via.note: the last town skirted before Limekilns

movements.routeNote: All night east along the north side of the Carse under the high line of the Ochils, giving Alloa, Clackmannan and Culross a wide berth, to the clachan of Limekilns on the Forth.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 26: "we walked through the north side of the Carse under the high line of the Ochil mountains; and by Alloa and Clackmannan and Culross, all of which we avoided... came to the little clachan of Limekilns."

movements.note: A Limekilns girl rows them over the Forth by night to the Lothian shore; David comes round to Queensferry to seek the lawyer Rankeillor.

via.name: the Hope
via.note: rowed across the narrows of the Forth by night

movements.routeNote: The Limekilns girl rows the two fugitives across the Hope - the narrows of the Forth - by night, landing them on the Lothian shore by Carriden, and David comes round to the Queen’s Ferry.
movements.routeSource: Stevenson, ch. 27: Limekilns "sits near in by the water-side, and looks across the Hope to the town of the Queensferry"; the girl rows them over to the far shore.

movements.note: By night David, Rankeillor and Alan go out to Shaws, and Alan lures Ebenezer into confessing he paid to have David kidnapped. The inheritance is won.
David and Alan part on the Corstorphine road, and David walks on alone into Edinburgh, to the doors of the bank and his fortune.

story.title: A June morning
story.narration: David Balfour, newly orphaned, leaves the quiet parish of Essendean one June morning in 1751. The minister hands him a letter: he has kin at the house of Shaws, near Cramond, and a fortune he never knew of.

story.title: To the house of Shaws
story.narration: Two days' walk out of the Borders brings him, footsore, to Shaws - not the mansion he imagined but a grim, half-built ruin, one wing roofless against the sky. The country folk cross themselves at its name.

story.title: Uncle Ebenezer
story.narration: Inside waits his uncle Ebenezer, a miser starved of company and hoarding secrets. That night he sends David up a broken stair in the dark, with no candle - a stair that ends in a sheer drop. Lightning saves the boy's neck.

story.title: Down to the ferry
story.narration: Ebenezer smooths it over, then walks David down to the Firth of Forth 'on a little business' with one Captain Hoseason. At the Hawes Inn by the Queen's Ferry, the trap is quietly set.

story.title: North about Scotland
story.narration: Coaxed aboard the brig Covenant, David is clubbed down and wakes bound in the hold, at sea, to be sold in the Carolinas. Shunning the Channel, Hoseason stands north about Scotland - round Cape Wrath and down the Hebrides toward the reefs off Mull.

story.title: The loss of the brig
story.narration: Mid-voyage the Covenant fishes out one Alan Breck Stewart, a Jacobite exile with a belt of gold, and David helps him fight off the crew. Then, steering blind, the brig strikes the Torran Rocks - and David is washed up alone on Earraid.

story.title: Across Mull
story.narration: For days he plays castaway on Earraid before grasping that the tide goes out and he can simply wade off. On Mull he sets out east across the long, crooked island on the trail of Alan, who left him a silver button as a token.

story.title: Over the Sound
story.narration: From Torosay he takes the ferry across the Sound of Mull to Kinlochaline, on the mainland shore of Morvern - a step nearer the Appin country, and nearer Alan.

story.title: Through Morvern
story.narration: He crosses the wild peninsula of Morvern and comes down at last to the shore of Loch Linnhe, near Kingairloch, with the Appin country lying just across the water.

story.title: The death of the Red Fox
story.narration: A boatman rows him over Loch Linnhe into Appin. In the wood of Lettermore he meets Colin Roy Campbell, the King's factor - the hated 'Red Fox' - and, that same minute, a shot cracks from the hillside and Campbell drops dead in the road.

story.title: Take to the heather
story.narration: David runs uphill after the vanished gunman and is at once cried out as an accomplice. A hand hauls him into the birches - Alan Breck again, ashore and armed. Now the two are hunted men together, and the flight in the heather begins.

story.title: The Heugh of Corrynakiegh
story.narration: They flee up into the Appin hills and lie hidden five days in the rocky cleft of Corrynakiegh, catching trout and lighting a signal fire for money and news, while redcoats comb the glens below for the Red Fox's killers.

story.title: The moor
story.narration: East to Rannoch Moor, a great skeleton-firred waste of bog. Under a blazing sun, in full sight of soldiers posted on the hills, they cross it on their bellies, crawling heather-bush to heather-bush until David is utterly spent.

story.title: Cluny's Cage
story.narration: Driven into the heart of Ben Alder, they are found by the scouts of Cluny Macpherson - a chief in hiding since Culloden - and taken up to his wattled hideaway, Cluny's Cage, slung among the trees on the mountainside.

story.title: The quarrel
story.narration: Alan gambles their money away to Cluny at cards, and on the long march south the two quarrel bitterly and nearly part. David falls sick; in a Balquhidder cottage they rest, and Alan out-pipes Robin Oig, a son of Rob Roy, sooner than fight him.

story.title: The bridge of Stirling
story.narration: Mended, David comes down out of the Highlands to Stirling, where the bridge is the main pass over the Forth - and held by a sentry. Alan's attempt to talk their way across fails outright, and they must think again.

story.title: The clachan of Limekilns
story.narration: They slip east under the Ochils to the Forth-side clachan of Limekilns. There a change-house girl, taking David for a hunted young gentleman, is moved to pity - and offers to row them over the water herself, by night.

story.title: Across the Forth
story.narration: By night the girl rows the two fugitives over the Forth to the Lothian shore. David comes round to the Queen's Ferry - where his troubles began - to seek out Mr Rankeillor, the family lawyer who holds the key to his inheritance.

story.title: The trap re-set
story.narration: Rankeillor believes David's tale and lays a scheme. That night all three go out to Shaws, and Alan, hidden in the dark, draws Ebenezer into boasting that he paid to have his nephew kidnapped. Caught by his own tongue, the old man yields up the estate.

story.title: Good-bye
story.narration: His fortune won, David parts from Alan on the Corstorphine road - Alan to find a ship for France, David on alone into Edinburgh, to the doors of the British Linen bank. Rich, safe, and strangely heavy-hearted.
